questions.questionText:
Classify each variable as **qualitative** or **quantitative**, and if quantitative, as **discrete** or **continuous**.
(a) The nationality of a student.
(b) The number of siblings a student has.
(c) The temperature at noon each day.
(d) The grade awarded in an examination (A, B, C, D, E).
questions.correctAnswer: (a) qualitative, (b) quantitative discrete, (c) quantitative continuous, (d) qualitative

markScheme.desc: (d) qualitative / categorical (grades are labels, not numbers)

workingSteps: (b) Number of siblings is counted in whole numbers → quantitative discrete
(c) Temperature can take any value → quantitative continuous
(d) Grades are labels, not numerical measurements → qualitative
questions.explanation: Qualitative = categories/labels. Quantitative = numbers. Discrete = counted; continuous = measured.

questions.context:
A researcher surveys 50 households about their energy use. She records the following variables:
- Type of heating system (gas/electric/other)
- Monthly electricity bill (£)
- Number of people in the household
- Whether they have solar panels (yes/no)

parts.label: a
parts.questionText: Identify all the quantitative variables from the list above.
parts.correctAnswer: Monthly electricity bill; Number of people in the household

markScheme.desc: Number of people in the household

workingSteps: Monthly electricity bill is measured in pounds → quantitative
Number of people is counted → quantitative

parts.label: b
parts.questionText: Explain the difference between discrete and continuous data, giving one example of each from the survey.
parts.correctAnswer: Discrete: number of people (only whole numbers). Continuous: electricity bill (any value in a range).

markScheme.desc: Discrete can only take specific/whole number values, e.g. number of people
Continuous can take any value in a range, e.g. electricity bill

parts.label: c
parts.questionText:
The researcher groups the electricity bills into classes: £0–£50, £50–£100, £100–£150, £150–£200.
Explain why grouping the data changes the type of data.
parts.correctAnswer: Grouping makes the data discrete/categorical as each value is assigned to one of a fixed set of groups

markScheme.desc: Grouped data is treated as discrete / each value placed into one of a fixed number of categories oe

parts.label: d
parts.questionText:
The relative frequency of households with gas heating is 0.56.
Calculate the number of households with gas heating.

questions.questionText:
A school records the following data for each student:
- Student ID number
- Height (cm)
- Number of absences
- House colour (Red/Blue/Green/Yellow)
- Score on a standardised test (0–100)

(a) Identify which variable is quantitative continuous. Give a reason.
(b) Explain why the student ID number should **not** be treated as quantitative data, even though it is a number.
(c) Two students have absence counts of 3 and 4. Explain why it is valid to say one student was absent exactly twice as often as another, but this comparison would not be valid for their heights.
questions.correctAnswer: (a) Height — measured and can take any value. (b) ID is a label with no numerical meaning. (c) Absence count has a true zero so ratios are meaningful; height zero is not meaningful in this context (ratio scale vs interval scale).

markScheme.desc: (a) Can take any value / is measured not counted oe
(b) ID is a label / arithmetic operations are meaningless / e.g. adding IDs has no meaning oe
(c) Absences have a true zero so ratio comparisons are valid
(c) Height ratios are not meaningful in the same way / height does not have a meaningful zero oe

workingSteps: (a) Height is measured continuously → quantitative continuous
(b) ID numbers are arbitrary labels; maths on them (mean, ratio) is meaningless
(c) 4 absences is exactly twice 2 absences (ratio scale). Heights cannot be compared as ratios since 0 cm height is meaningless.
questions.explanation: Distinguishing nominal (label), ordinal, interval, and ratio scales is important for choosing appropriate statistical methods.
